executive_summary: The US dollar trades at DXY 100.5, a 10-month high driven by safe-haven demand from the Iran-US war ('Operation Epic Fury,' launched February 28) and an oil shock that pushed WTI above $100/bbl and Core PCE to 3.1% YoY — the highest in nearly two years. DXY has rallied +2.8% in March, its best monthly gain since late 2024, recovering from the September 2025 trough of 95.55 but still trading 3.4% below its April 2025 Liberation Day high of 104.39. Performance is bifurcated: USD has strengthened sharply over 1-3 months on conflict-driven flows but remains weaker over 12 months (DXY -3.4% YoY) as the broader structural bearish thesis — post-peak US exceptionalism, Fed easing cycle, twin deficits — has merely been interrupted, not invalidated. The BIS REER at 106.9 and IMF staff assessment of 10.7% overvaluation confirm the dollar remains fundamentally expensive, yet the war premium, re-accelerating core inflation, and hawkish Fed repricing (bond markets now pricing a possible rate hike) create a powerful near-term floor. The central tension is between the cyclical safe-haven bid that dominates the tactical horizon and the structural overvaluation plus eventual geopolitical de-escalation that dominates the strategic horizon. Our headline stance: Tactically Bullish USD with moderate conviction (3/5), strategically Bearish USD with moderate-to-high conviction (3/5), reflecting a conflict-inflated currency trading above fair value that will weaken once the geopolitical premium fades.

bis_reer.interpretation: USD REER at 106.94 (Jan 2026, Index 2020=100) sits approximately 7% above its 2020 baseline and in the 72nd percentile of its post-2000 distribution. The IMF staff assessment finds 10.7% overvaluation (midpoint), while TIAA estimates 18% above long-term REER average.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The Federal Reserve Trade-Weighted Broad USD Index at 120.89 remains elevated by historical standards but has declined from the November 2025 peak of 115.39 on the Real Broad Index, confirming the structural downtrend that was interrupted by the Iran conflict.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: The market has priced the US fiscal trajectory and twin deficits through elevated term premium and bear curve steepening, but has not fully priced the magnitude of REER overvaluation. The conflict premium accounts for approximately 3-5% of current DXY levels — removing it would bring DXY to 96-97, closer to OECD PPP-implied fair values. The OECD PPP for EUR/USD at 1.1502 is essentially at current spot, suggesting EUR/USD is near fair value even with the conflict premium.
valuation.actionability: The valuation signal is structural, not actionable at the tactical horizon. REER overvaluation can persist for years. However, it establishes a gravitational pull that becomes increasingly relevant at the 3-6 month horizon, particularly once the conflict premium fades.

market_context.recent_regime: The USD is trading in a war-premium regime where safe-haven demand overwhelms valuation, positioning, and structural flow considerations. The Iran conflict has produced a classic dollar smile dynamic — USD strengthening simultaneously on risk-off sentiment and US economic resilience relative to energy-importing peers. Volatility has surged across asset classes (VIX 30+, WTI IV 51%), compressing time horizons and forcing a tactical focus. The market narrative has shifted from 'soft landing + Fed cuts = weaker USD' to 'stagflation risk + geopolitical premium = stronger USD,' though most institutional forecasters view this shift as temporary.

tactical.analysis: The Fed held at 3.50-3.75% on March 18 with a near-unanimous 11-1 vote (only Miran dissented for a cut), projecting a single 25bp cut for 2026 via the median dot — unchanged from December but with sharply upgraded inflation projections (Core PCE to 2.7% from 2.5%). Bond markets have gone further: 2Y yields at 3.89% have punched through the effective fed funds rate for the first time since November 2023, with some probability of a rate hike exceeding 50% on March 27 before settling back. This hawkish repricing is USD-supportive because it widens or maintains the rate differential advantage the US holds over most G10 peers. The Fed Funds midpoint of 3.625% ranks 4th in G10+SGD (behind AUD 4.10%, NOK 4.00%, GBP 3.75%), but USD retains significant carry over funding currencies: +288bp vs JPY, +363bp vs CHF, +163bp vs EUR, and +188bp vs SEK. The May 5-6 FOMC is approximately 95% priced for a hold, meaning no near-term catalyst for USD weakness from the rate channel. The gap between the dot plot median (one cut to 3.375% by year-end) and market pricing (no cuts or a possible hike) creates upside risk for USD if data forces the Fed to abandon even the single projected cut. With March NFP due April 3 and March CPI on April 10, the data flow over the next 2-4 weeks will either validate the hawkish repricing (supporting USD) or force a dovish recalibration (capping it). The carry trade is working in the current regime — elevated vol has not overwhelmed the yield advantage because the vol is driven by geopolitics rather than rate uncertainty, and the USD is on the right side of the dollar smile.

tactical.what_is_priced: Markets are pricing the Fed on hold through June with ~40% probability of a June cut. This feels about right given the data — Core PCE at 3.1% makes a cut unlikely before Q3 at the earliest, but the market has not fully committed to higher for longer either. The mispricing is modest: if March CPI surprises to the upside (driven by oil pass-through and tariff effects), the June cut probability will collapse toward 10-15%, providing an additional 15-20bp of USD support via front-end rates.

strategic.analysis: The strategic rate picture for USD is less supportive because the direction of travel for most G10 central banks is now toward tightening rather than easing, compressing differentials. The RBA has hiked twice (to 4.10%), Norges Bank has explicitly signaled imminent hikes, the BoJ's hawkish Summary of Opinions discussed larger-than-usual increases, and markets are pricing 3+ RBNZ hikes and 1-2 ECB hikes by year-end. This represents a dramatic reversal from the pre-conflict consensus of synchronized global easing. While the Fed's dot plot still projects one cut, the global rate trajectory is converging upward toward the US — meaning the Fed's rate advantage is eroding, not expanding, at the strategic horizon. The UST-Bund spread at +128bp has already compressed from +180bp in late 2025, and further convergence toward +80-100bp would remove a key EUR/USD support pillar. The UST-JGB spread at +201bp is narrowing as BoJ normalizes; if BoJ reaches 1.25% by year-end (plausible given the hawkish Takata dissent), the spread would compress to approximately 150bp, reducing USD/JPY's carry appeal. The 2s10s steepening to +48bp reflects growing term premium driven by fiscal concerns ($39T debt, Moody's downgrade, $10T+ rollover), which historically correlates with eventual USD weakness as foreign buyers demand more compensation. The Kevin Warsh Chair confirmation process introduces policy uncertainty — Warsh is perceived as more hawkish than Powell, which could initially support USD but ultimately constrain the Fed's ability to respond to a slowdown. The Fed's own dot plot trajectory (3.375% end-2026, 3.125% end-2027) implies gradual easing, while other G10 CBs are pivoting to hikes — a narrowing differential that is strategically bearish for USD.

If the differential compression plays out as expected, the carry advantage that currently supports USD will erode by 50-100bp across key pairs by Q3-Q4, removing a pillar of support.

The Fed is on hold while the BoJ, RBA, Norges Bank, and potentially the ECB are hiking — a directional divergence that matters more at the strategic horizon where rate path expectations dominate over current levels.

tactical.analysis: The US inflation picture has turned hawkish for the Fed's preferred measure, creating a near-term USD-supportive dynamic through two channels: delayed rate cuts and rising real rate expectations. Core PCE at 3.1% YoY (January 2026) is the highest in nearly two years and moving in the wrong direction — up from 2.8% in November. The 3-month annualized core PCE of approximately 3.66% is nearly double the 2% target. While headline CPI at 2.4% looks benign, the CPI-PCE divergence (driven by healthcare and financial services weighting differences) understates the inflation problem as seen by the Fed. The oil shock from Iran (WTI $100+, gas prices up more than $1/gallon in a month) will mechanically push March headline CPI higher, likely to 2.8-3.0% when reported on April 10, creating a USD-supportive headline. ISM Prices Paid at 70.5 (highest since June 2022) and S&P Global flash PMI showing selling prices at fastest increase since August 2022 confirm the supply-side price impulse is intensifying. Growth data is mixed but not collapsing: Q4 GDP was revised down to +0.7% SAAR (from +1.4% advance) due to government shutdown effects, but ISM Manufacturing at 52.4 and ISM Services at 56.1 remain expansionary. The labor market crack (NFP -92K, unemployment 4.4%) is concerning but initial claims at 210K suggest no immediate deterioration. Consumer sentiment (UMich at 53.3, 1st percentile) is abysmal but has historically been a poor leading indicator for growth. The tactical read: inflation data supports delayed cuts, which is USD-supportive; growth data is soft but not recessionary, which is insufficient to force the Fed's hand.

tactical.what_is_priced: Markets expect March CPI (April 10) to show an acceleration in headline driven by energy. Core CPI is expected to remain around 2.5-2.6%. The potential surprise would be in core measures — if tariff pass-through (apparel +1.3% in February) combines with energy-driven services inflation to push core CPI above 2.7%, that would be a hawkish surprise supporting USD. The February PCE release (April 9) will be scrutinized for whether core PCE stayed at or above 3.1%.

strategic.analysis: The strategic inflation-growth picture for USD is ambiguous — the classic stagflation setup where high inflation supports rates but deteriorating growth undermines demand for US assets. Core PCE at 3.1% will keep the Fed on hold or hawkish through mid-2026, but the growth trajectory is worrying. GDP decelerated from +4.4% (Q3 2025) to +0.7% (Q4 2025), and Atlanta Fed GDPNow is tracking +2.0% for Q1 — a slowdown path. The labor market is softening: NFP has averaged just +5,700 per month over the past three months, the weakest since the 2020 recovery. The S&P Global flash PMI for March at 51.4 with declining employment signals the early stages of a growth downturn, exacerbated by the oil shock's hit to consumer spending and business confidence (UMich at 53.3, Conference Board expectations below 80 for 13+ months). If this develops into a genuine growth scare (Q2 GDP below 1%, unemployment above 4.5%), the Fed would face a brutal policy dilemma: cut rates to support growth (bearish for USD carry but potentially bullish for risk sentiment) or hold rates to fight inflation (supportive for USD carry but risking a deeper slowdown). Neither outcome is clearly USD-positive at the strategic horizon. The G10 macro comparison shows the US still leads on GDP growth (2.0% YoY vs 0.5% Japan, 1.0% UK/Switzerland), which provides a floor, but the direction of the growth differential is converging rather than diverging as the oil shock disproportionately hits non-commodity importers (JPY, EUR, GBP) while the US benefits from relative energy self-sufficiency. The neutral stance reflects this genuine uncertainty.

Conversely, if growth stabilizes at 1.5-2.0% while inflation remains elevated, the higher for longer narrative strengthens, which is neutral-to-supportive.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical-strategic shift from Bullish to Neutral reflects the inflation momentum supporting USD in the near term giving way to growth deterioration that creates uncertainty over 3-6 months. Inflation is the dominant force at the tactical horizon (weight 0.18), but the strategic horizon requires assessing whether the growth slowdown overwhelms the inflation support — and that remains genuinely uncertain.

tactical.analysis: The risk environment is firmly risk-off and USD is the primary beneficiary through the dollar smile framework. The VIX at 30.6 sits in the upper quintile of its historical range, driven by the Iran war, oil shock, and equity selloff (S&P 500 down 8.7% from January highs, five consecutive weekly declines — longest since 2022). Credit spreads are widening: US HY OAS at 321bp (up from 284bp in February) and IG estimated at 85-95bp. The AAII sentiment survey shows 49.8% bearish (hit 52.0% on March 18 — extreme pessimism). The CBOE SPX put/call ratio at 1.21 reflects heavy institutional hedging. This is a classic risk-off regime where the USD benefits as the global reserve currency and safe haven — the left side of the dollar smile. The correlation regime is intact: USD strengthening correlates with equity weakness, rising VIX, and widening credit spreads, exactly as the framework predicts for risk-off episodes. The Dow has entered correction territory, and recession probability estimates are rising (Moody's 49%). The key question is whether risk-off intensifies or stabilizes. Near-term catalysts for intensification include any escalation in the Iran conflict (Strait of Hormuz full closure, Hezbollah/Houthi expansion), while stabilization catalysts include ceasefire progress (Trump looking to end Iran war as of March 31). The risk balance over 2-4 weeks favors continued elevated risk aversion, supporting USD.

tactical.what_is_priced: The VIX at 30+ suggests markets are pricing continued uncertainty but not a tail-risk crisis. VIX above 40 would signal crisis-level fear; we are in the elevated anxiety zone. If the Iran conflict escalates to full Strait of Hormuz closure (oil to $130-150/bbl), VIX could spike to 40-50 and DXY would likely test 104+. This tail scenario is partially priced (~20% probability embedded in options markets) but not fully hedged.

strategic.analysis: The strategic risk outlook is genuinely uncertain because the conflict trajectory is unknowable. If the Iran war resolves via ceasefire (Trump has signaled willingness; talks are reportedly productive), risk appetite would recover sharply — VIX would normalize to 15-20, equities would rally, credit spreads would compress, and USD would lose its risk-off bid as the dollar smile shifts back to the goldilocks middle (where USD weakens). This is the base case scenario that most bank forecasters are implicitly assuming when they project DXY at 93-98 by year-end. However, if the conflict escalates or persists through Q2-Q3, the risk-off regime would continue supporting USD. The S&P 500's forward P/E at 19.7 (below 5-year average of 20.1) suggests some risk premium is priced but valuations are not at distressed levels — there is room for further downside if earnings expectations deteriorate. The strategic risk assessment must also consider that risk-off USD strength creates its own headwinds: a stronger USD hurts US exporters, tightens financial conditions for EM borrowers, and contributes to the growth slowdown that could eventually undermine USD support. This self-correcting mechanism limits the duration of risk-off USD rallies historically — the 2020 COVID spike lasted 6 weeks, the 2022 energy crisis drove USD for 9 months. At the 3-6 month horizon, risk normalization is more likely than not (60/40), hence Neutral.

tactical.analysis: Positioning has undergone a dramatic shift in March, moving from net USD shorts to net USD longs at an unprecedented pace. The $11 billion of net USD buying in the week to March 17 was the largest since June 2018, flipping the aggregate speculative community to +$6.3 billion long. EUR positioning saw the largest weekly long liquidation on record (84K contracts), slashing net longs from +180K to approximately +9K over five weeks. JPY shorts are at 20-month highs, and AUD longs are at 9-year highs. For the USD specifically, the current net long positioning at approximately 40th percentile of historical max is notable for speed but not yet at extreme levels — the 2022 peak was roughly 2x larger. The speed of the move creates a tactical pause signal: when positioning shifts this rapidly, the easy money has been made and the risk of mean-reversion increases. However, positioning momentum can be self-reinforcing in conflict-driven regimes — the pain trade for EUR shorts and JPY shorts would be a squeeze, but without a catalyst (ceasefire, dovish Fed surprise), these positions tend to extend rather than reverse. Open interest across major pairs is elevated (EUR 764K, JPY 328K, AUD 258K), indicating high market engagement. The Neutral stance reflects the tension between positioning momentum (still favoring further USD longs) and the contrarian warning from the speed of repositioning.

tactical.what_is_priced: The rapid USD buying suggests the conflict premium is largely priced into positioning. Further USD strength would require either conflict escalation or additional hawkish data surprises to pull more capital into USD longs. The risk is asymmetric from here: if the catalyst fades (ceasefire), the unwind of these positions would produce a sharp USD reversal (the EUR's record liquidation creates enormous snap-back potential).

strategic.analysis: At the strategic horizon, positioning becomes a stronger contrarian signal because the time available for mean-reversion to operate is sufficient. The aggregate USD long of $6.3 billion, if it extends further toward the 2022 highs (~$15-20B), would create a crowded trade that historically precedes multi-month USD weakness. More critically, the EUR's positioning crash from 90th to 10th percentile has created enormous underweight exposure that will normalize over 3-6 months as funds rebuild structural EUR positions. The BoJ normalization cycle will eventually trigger JPY short-covering — the current 85th percentile short positioning in JPY is unsustainable if BoJ hikes to 1.00-1.25% by year-end. AUD longs at 9-year highs are the exception — these reflect fundamentals (RBA hiking, commodity exports) rather than speculation and are less contrarian. Real money flows (pension funds, reserve managers, SWFs) have been diversifying away from USD for years; IMF COFER data shows a gradual decline in USD's share of global reserves from approximately 60% in 2020 to approximately 57% in 2025. This structural de-dollarization flow is slow but persistent at the strategic horizon. The strategic positioning read is bearish: current USD longs are conflict-driven and temporary; underlying structural flows favor diversification away from USD.

factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Positioning is not yet at extreme USD-long levels (~40th percentile), so the contrarian signal is moderate rather than strong. However, the speed of the repositioning is itself extreme and historically precedes 2-4 week consolidation phases. The EUR's crash from 90th to 10th percentile creates the strongest contrarian signal in G10: if EUR/USD stabilizes or rallies even modestly, the covering flow could amplify the move. JPY shorts at 85th percentile are similarly vulnerable to a squeeze if BoJ delivers a surprise hike or if risk-off intensifies enough to trigger carry unwinds. The contrarian case against USD longs: positions were built on a conflict bid that has a finite duration — once the war narrative shifts from escalation to negotiation, these positions reverse. Historical analogues (Russia-Ukraine February 2022, Gulf War 1990) show USD conflict bids last 4-8 weeks on average before fading; we are now at day 32.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: The strategic contrarian case is clearer: USD longs built on a war premium will unwind as geopolitical uncertainty fades. The EUR's record liquidation creates a spring-loaded snap-back effect — when EUR buying resumes, the move will be amplified by the low base. JPY short-covering, if triggered by a BoJ hike to 1.00%, would be violent given the 85th percentile positioning and the carry unwind mechanics (leveraged carry trades reverse faster than they build). The probability of a significant positioning-driven USD selloff within 3-6 months is 55-60%.

tactical.analysis: The US current account deficit of approximately -2.4% of GDP is a structural USD headwind, but it is the second widest in G10 only to NZ (-5.5%) and not dramatically worse than the UK (-1.6%) or Canada (-1.5%). The current account is a slow-moving variable that generates limited tactical signal. The terms of trade picture is ambiguous for the US: oil above $100/bbl has a mildly positive direct effect on USD (US is now roughly energy self-sufficient due to shale production), but the net effect is more complex because higher energy prices suppress global growth (negative for US exports) and create fiscal drag through higher government energy costs. The commodity exposure rule for USD (WTI crude, Henry Hub gas, GSCI agriculture — mixed) confirms the ambiguous relationship. The trade policy landscape is in flux: the Section 122 universal 10% tariff generates revenue ($421B projected for FY2027) and has a modest current account improving effect through import suppression, but it simultaneously invites retaliation (China investigating US trade practices) and disrupts supply chains. The SCOTUS IEEPA tariff ruling and $166B refund requirement is a near-term fiscal drag. Trading partner concentration matters: the top 3 US partners are Canada (~18% of trade), Mexico (~16%), and China (~14%). The Section 122 tariff applies to all three, and the USMCA joint review (starting July 1, 2026) is a medium-term risk. For the tactical horizon, the external balance is not generating a directional signal — USD is being driven by risk flows and rate expectations, not trade flows.
key_data_points: Current account: -2.4% of GDP (2nd widest in G10 after NZ)
WTI crude: ~$102/bbl; Brent: ~$112/bbl (oil shock from Iran conflict)
Section 122 tariff: 10% universal (150-day limit; expires ~July 2026)
Average effective tariff rate: 13.7% (February 2026)
Top trading partners: Canada (~18%), Mexico (~16%), China (~14%)
SCOTUS tariff ruling: $166B refund required (fiscal drag)
tactical.what_is_priced: The current account deficit is structural and fully priced — no tactical edge. The oil price surge has a near-neutral net effect on USD at current levels. The tactical signal is flat.

strategic.analysis: At the strategic horizon, the external balance factor becomes significantly more bearish for USD. The twin deficits (current account -2.4% GDP, fiscal deficit -5.8% GDP) require continuous foreign capital inflows to finance, and the willingness of foreign investors to fund these deficits at current yield levels is not guaranteed. The IMF's overvaluation assessment (10.7%) is itself a function of the external imbalance — the USD must be structurally overvalued to suppress imports and attract the capital needed to finance the deficit. The terms of trade picture deteriorates strategically if the Iran conflict resolves: a return to $70-80/bbl oil would remove the mild USD benefit from energy self-sufficiency while boosting commodity-exporting competitors (CAD, NOK, AUD). The tariff landscape is strategically uncertain: Section 122 expires after 150 days (~July 2026) without Congressional action, and there is no clear path to renewal. If tariffs lapse, import volumes would surge, widening the trade deficit. The reserve currency dynamics are slowly eroding USD's structural advantage: COFER data shows USD's share declining, and central banks have accelerated gold purchases as an alternative reserve asset. China's revised Foreign Trade Law and investigations into US trade practices signal a strategic decoupling that will reduce bilateral trade volumes — historically, declining US-China trade has been associated with a weaker USD because China recycles fewer trade surpluses into UST purchases. The structural flow picture is unambiguously negative for USD at the 3-6 month horizon, and it receives the highest weight (0.16) of any factor at the strategic horizon.

IMF REER overvaluation: 10.7% (staff midpoint)
COFER USD reserve share: ~57% (declining from 60% in 2020)
Section 122 tariff expiry: ~July 2026 (150-day limit from February)
Surplus economies (structural USD sellers): SGD +19%, NOK +13%, CHF +8%, SEK +5.5%
USMCA joint review: starts July 1, 2026
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has largely priced the current account deficit as structural, but may be underpricing two risks: (1) the tariff expiry in July, which would widen the trade deficit, and (2) the acceleration of reserve diversification if the Warsh-led Fed is perceived as more politically compromised. The strategic edge is in the structural flow: approximately $500B+ of annual current account deficit requires continuous foreign financing, and any reduction in foreign appetite (geopolitical diversification, higher yields elsewhere from global tightening) would pressure USD.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical Neutral reflects the external balance's limited near-term signal power, while the strategic Bearish captures the weight of structural twin deficits, reserve diversification, and tariff expiry risk. The external balance is the quintessential slow-moving factor that dominates at strategic horizons.

tactical.analysis: The March 18 FOMC communication was hawkish relative to pre-meeting expectations and is tactically USD-supportive. Powell's press conference struck a decidedly patient tone: if we don't see progress, then you won't see that rate cut is the clearest conditional guidance the Fed has issued in months. The addition of the implications of developments in the Middle East for the U.S. economy are uncertain to the statement signals the Fed is using the conflict as justification to delay action in either direction. The dot plot retained the one-cut median for 2026 but the distribution shifted hawkish: 14 of 19 dots clustered at no cut or one cut, up from 13 in December. The lone dissenter, Governor Miran (for a cut), was offset by Governor Waller's hawkish pivot from his January dovish stance. The 11-1 vote (vs 10-2 in January) signals a more unified hawkish front. The gap between the dot plot median (3.375% year-end = one cut) and market pricing (flat to slight hike probability) is modest — roughly 0-25bp. This small gap limits the surprise potential from upcoming Fed communication. The Fed's credibility remains high: markets trust Powell's data-dependent framework, and the Fed has followed through on its guidance over the past 18 months (delivering the three cuts it signaled in September 2025). However, the Chair transition to Kevin Warsh introduces tactical uncertainty.
key_data_points: March 18 FOMC: Hold, 11-1 (Miran dissent for cut)
Dot plot: 14/19 dots at 0-1 cuts (hawkish shift from 13/19 in Dec)
Market-implied vs dots gap: ~0-25bp (small; limited surprise potential)
Next FOMC: May 5-6, 2026; may be Powell's last as Chair
Warsh nomination: Senate confirmation pending; perceived as more hawkish
March 18 FOMC minutes: due April 8, 2026
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has fully absorbed the March FOMC's hawkish message. The tactical edge from guidance is small — the remaining source of surprise would be the April 8 FOMC minutes (if they reveal a more serious discussion of rate hikes) or any public remarks from Fed governors suggesting hike consideration. Powell's post-FOMC speeches are unlikely to deviate from the press conference message.

strategic.analysis: The strategic guidance picture is complicated by the Chair transition. Kevin Warsh's likely succession (Senate confirmation expected by May-June) introduces genuine uncertainty about the Fed's reaction function. Warsh is perceived as more hawkish than Powell on inflation but more sympathetic to executive branch pressure on rates — a combination that makes forward guidance less predictable. If Warsh takes over as Chair, markets will go through a calibration period where the new Chair's credibility must be established through actions, not just words. Historically, Chair transitions have created 2-4 months of elevated FX volatility as markets reassess the policy function (e.g., Yellen to Powell 2018, Powell's own rocky start with the December 2018 hike). The Fed's credibility on inflation fighting remains intact — the commitment to 2% is not in question — but the strategic guidance channel is clouded by the Warsh transition, uncertainty about how the Fed would respond to stagflation (conflicting mandates), and political pressure on monetary policy (DOJ subpoenas, Trump's public comments on rates). At the global level, the Fed's guidance advantage over other G10 CBs is diminishing: the BoJ is becoming more transparent under Ueda, the Riksbank publishes named minutes, and the RBNZ/Norges Bank publish rate paths.

BoJ credibility improving: Ueda regime more transparent than Kuroda-era

strategic.what_is_priced: The market is pricing some transition uncertainty (VIX elevated, options expensive) but may be underestimating the calibration period for a Warsh-led Fed. If Warsh's early communications are perceived as politically influenced or unpredictable, USD could weaken on institutional credibility concerns — a risk that is not well-priced given markets' assumption of continuity.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical Bullish stance reflects the clear, hawkish March FOMC communication supporting USD in the near term. The strategic Neutral reflects the Chair transition uncertainty that clouds guidance credibility over 3-6 months. The inflection point is Warsh's confirmation and first public communications as Chair.

tactical.analysis: The Iran-US war is the dominant event risk in global markets and is unambiguously USD-supportive at the tactical horizon. Operation Epic Fury (launched February 28) has entered its 33rd day with no ceasefire. Over 5,000 targets struck, Iran's naval capability neutralized, and the Strait of Hormuz partially blocked — driving WTI above $100/bbl and Brent above $112/bbl. The conflict has expanded: Hezbollah attacked Israel (triggering the 2026 Lebanon war), Houthis threatened intervention, and Kuwait ports were attacked. The USD benefits through three channels: safe-haven demand, oil-driven inflation supporting Fed hawkishness, and relative US energy self-sufficiency vs import-dependent G10 peers (JPY, EUR, GBP). Near-term risks are asymmetrically tilted: escalation (Strait full closure leading to oil $130-150/bbl and DXY 104+) is a realistic tail scenario with approximately 15-20% probability, while de-escalation (ceasefire leading to oil $70-80 and DXY 96-98) is plausible but unlikely within 2-4 weeks given the military momentum. Trump has signaled willingness to negotiate (looking to end Iran war March 31; productive talks March 22; 5-day strike pause March 23) but the Pentagon is simultaneously developing final blow options. The April 2 Liberation Day anniversary passes without new tariff action. None of the upcoming data events (NFP April 3, CPI April 10) are likely to override the Iran conflict as the dominant USD driver.

WTI crude: ~$102/bbl; Brent: ~$112/bbl
Strait of Hormuz: partially blocked; Mojtaba Khamenei vowed closure
US gas prices: ~$3.99/gal (up >$1/gal in one month)
Trump signals: looking to end Iran war (Mar 31); 5-day strike pause (Mar 23)
Tail risk: Strait full closure = oil $130-150 = DXY 104+ (15-20% probability)
War Powers Resolution failed 212-219 in House
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing continued conflict with modest de-escalation hope (oil at $102 rather than crisis levels of $130+; VIX at 30 rather than 40+). A surprise ceasefire announcement would trigger a rapid 3-5% DXY decline as risk premia unwind. Conversely, Strait full closure would add another 2-4% to DXY. The risk-reward for USD from the event risk factor is asymmetrically positive at the tactical horizon because the conflict premium is already partially priced but escalation scenarios are under-hedged.

strategic.analysis: The strategic event risk assessment must incorporate the high probability that the Iran conflict resolves or de-escalates within 3-6 months. Historical analogues for US-initiated military campaigns suggest active combat phases of 4-12 weeks before transitioning to negotiation or occupation phases with lower market impact (e.g., Iraq 2003: 3-week invasion followed by price normalization over 2 months). The US's stated objective appears to be regime change/nuclear disarmament rather than prolonged occupation, which implies a finite conflict duration. Trump's repeated ceasefire signals suggest domestic political pressure (33% approval, rising gas prices) is creating incentives to negotiate. If the conflict ends by June-July, oil would return to $70-85/bbl, removing the inflation impulse, safe-haven bid, and energy-vulnerability differential that currently support USD. However, non-conflict event risks remain: the Section 122 tariff expires after 150 days (~July 2026), Trump-Xi summit (expected May) could produce breakthroughs or breakdowns in US-China trade relations, the 2026 midterm election cycle begins shaping fiscal and trade policy by summer, Russia-Ukraine remains unresolved, and Taiwan risk is elevated (most extensive PLA drills in December 2025). The strategic event landscape is too uncertain for a directional call — multiple scenarios with divergent USD implications are plausible.
key_data_points: Historical analogue: Iraq 2003 active phase ~3 weeks; market normalization ~2 months
Trump approval: 33% (record second-term low) — pressure to end conflict
Section 122 tariff expiry: ~July 2026
Trump-Xi summit: expected May 2026
Russia-Ukraine: no ceasefire; peace talks suspended
Taiwan: elevated PLA activity (26 aircraft March 15)
2026 midterm elections: November 3; generic ballot D+6
strategic.what_is_priced: The market (via bank forecasts of DXY 93-98 by year-end) is implicitly pricing conflict resolution by mid-2026, which seems reasonable given historical patterns and political incentives. If the conflict persists through Q3, these forecasts would need revision upward by 3-5 points. The strategic uncertainty is genuinely high (confidence 40) — this factor is a coin flip between USD-positive persistence and USD-negative resolution.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish (80% confidence) reflects the active, escalating conflict's strong USD support over 2-4 weeks. Strategic Neutral (40% confidence) reflects the high probability of de-escalation within 3-6 months but genuine uncertainty about timing and residual event risks.

tactical.analysis: US fiscal dynamics are worrying at the structural level but not generating a tactical directional signal. The total public debt crossed $39 trillion in March, with debt-to-GDP at approximately 100-101%. The FY2026 projected deficit of $1.9 trillion (5.8% of GDP) is among the highest peacetime levels in US history. Moody's downgraded the US from Aaa to Aa1 in May 2025, completing the loss of the last perfect credit rating — the first time the US has had no AAA/Aaa rating from any major agency. The 10Y UST term premium at +59bp (Kim-Wright, January 2026) and +62bp (ACM, NY Fed) is elevated by historical standards, consistent with markets demanding more compensation for fiscal risk. Treasury issuance is heavy ($606B in one week during March) and $10+ trillion must be rolled over in 2026. However, bond markets are functioning normally — auctions are well-received, no failed auctions, and no sudden foreign buyer retreat. The government shutdown (partial, DHS) is ongoing but its market impact is limited. For the tactical horizon, fiscal risks are background noise — the market has priced the US's fiscal trajectory and is not actively punishing it through yields (which are rising on inflation expectations, not sovereign risk).
key_data_points: Fiscal deficit: $1.9T projected FY2026 (5.8% of GDP)
Sovereign ratings: S&P AA+, Moody's Aa1, Fitch AA+ (no triple-A rating)
10Y term premium: +59bp Kim-Wright; +62bp ACM
Treasury issuance: $606B in one week (March); $10T+ rollover in 2026
CBO 2026-2036 cumulative deficit: $24.4T (6.1% avg)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced the US fiscal trajectory through elevated term premium and bear curve steepening. No further tactical repricing expected unless a debt ceiling crisis emerges or a major rating agency places the US on negative watch.

strategic.analysis: At the strategic horizon, fiscal dynamics become more USD-negative through three channels. First, the absolute level of deficit spending ($1.9T) requires continuous foreign capital inflows that are not guaranteed as global central banks tighten policy (offering competitive yields to USTs for the first time in years — UK Gilts at 4.89%, ACGBs at 5.00%, NZGBs at 4.89% all yield more than USTs). Second, the term premium expansion (+59bp and rising) reflects growing investor skepticism about fiscal sustainability — if term premium reaches +100bp (plausible given $39T debt + rising issuance), it would add approximately 40bp to 10Y yields without any change in short rates, steepening the curve further and eventually weighing on growth. Third, the political landscape is shifting: the midterms (November 2026) and the expiration of TCJA provisions create fiscal uncertainty that discourages long-duration UST investment by foreign official institutions. The CBO's projection of 6.1% average deficits over 2026-2036 is structurally incompatible with a strong USD — at some point, the currency must depreciate to reduce the real value of these obligations or attract capital at cheaper valuations. The strategic fiscal case is bearish for USD, but it operates through a slow-moving channel and is partially offset by the no alternative reserve currency argument.
key_data_points: CBO 10-year deficit: $24.4T cumulative (6.1% avg)
Foreign competition for capital: Gilts 4.89%, ACGBs 5.00% now yield MORE than USTs
$10T+ Treasury rollover in 2026 at elevated yields
Moody's downgrade: May 2025 (from Aaa to Aa1)
Interest expense: ~3.5% of GDP and rising (approaching defense spending)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market has partially priced fiscal risk through term premium but is likely underpricing the risk of a buyer's strike if global tightening continues and alternative sovereign yields remain competitive. The edge: if foreign official institutions reduce UST purchases by even 5-10% at the margin, the required term premium increase would be significant (additional 20-30bp), pressuring USD.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Neutral reflects the market's ability to absorb fiscal concerns in the near term without repricing USD. Strategic Bearish reflects the growing structural challenge of financing twin deficits at the 3-6 month horizon, especially as foreign yield alternatives become increasingly competitive.

tactical.analysis: The DXY's technical structure has turned bullish following the March breakout above the 50-day and 200-day moving averages. Price at 100.5 now sits above all three major MAs (50d ~98.5, 100d ~98.0, 200d ~99.0) in a bullish stacked configuration that typically attracts systematic/CTA buying flows. The MA crossover — 50d crossing above 200d — is a classical golden cross signal that, while lagging, confirms trend-following capital will be positioned long USD. Support is well-defined at 100.00 (psychological), 99.0 (200d MA), and 96.0-97.0 (major shelf). Resistance at 101.98-102.00 is the critical overhead level — a break above would confirm a new bullish regime and likely trigger momentum buying toward 104. The vol regime is elevated but trending: DXY IV at 6.84-7.21% (annualized) trades at a premium to estimated realized vol of 5.5-6.5%, producing an IV premium of approximately 0.5-1.5 ppts. This means options are slightly expensive and the market is pricing event risk (Iran escalation) that has not fully materialized in price action. EUR/USD implied vol at ~7.5% and USD/JPY IV at ~10% reflect the differentiated vol landscape across pairs. Risk reversals are likely biased toward EUR puts (negative EUR/USD RR) and near-flat on USD/JPY (safe-haven bid vs BoJ risk). The vol term structure is likely in mild backwardation (near-term vol elevated from conflict uncertainty), consistent with event-driven regimes.

tactical.what_is_priced: The IV premium suggests the market is pricing a 10-15% probability of a significant vol event (Strait closure, military escalation) beyond what has already occurred. If the conflict stabilizes without further escalation, this vol premium will decay — favoring short-vol or spot-based USD expressions over options. If escalation occurs, the vol premium is justified and would expand.

strategic.analysis: The strategic market structure picture is less clear than the tactical. While the DXY has broken above the 200d MA, this breakout was driven by an exogenous shock (war) rather than fundamental re-rating, and war-driven breakouts have historically lower persistence than fundamental ones. The 2022 analogy is instructive: DXY broke above the 200d MA on the Russia-Ukraine invasion and maintained the trend for 7 months — but that was reinforced by a hawkish Fed hiking 425bp. The current cycle lacks that fundamental reinforcement (Fed is on hold, not hiking aggressively). If the conflict resolves, the DXY would likely retrace to the 96-98 range where the 200d MA cluster existed before the March rally, unwinding the technical breakout. The vol regime is likely to remain elevated for 2-3 months (conflict uncertainty) before normalizing, which means trend-following strategies (CTA flows) will remain engaged but could reverse sharply if vol collapses. The 102 resistance level is the key structural arbiter: if DXY fails there and reverses below 100, the March breakout was a false signal and the broader bearish structure (descending from 108 in Q4 2024) reasserts.
key_data_points: 200d MA breakout: war-driven (lower persistence than fundamental breakouts)
Key structural level: 102.00 — break above confirms new regime; failure confirms spike-and-fade
VIX normalization timeline: 2-3 months if conflict resolves (from 30 to 18-22)

CTA positioning: likely net long USD following MA breakout; would reverse on retrace below 200d MA
strategic.what_is_priced: The technical breakout has attracted momentum capital (CTA flows), but the sustainability is uncertain. A DXY failure at 102 and retrace below 99.5 (200d MA) would trigger CTA selling, amplifying the reversal. This creates a binary structural setup at the strategic horizon: break above 102 equals bullish confirmation; fail at 102 equals bearish reversal.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: The tactical Bullish reflects the confirmed MA breakout and bullish stacked configuration supporting near-term upside momentum. The strategic Neutral reflects the uncertainty about whether the war-driven breakout persists or fades, with 102 as the critical adjudicator.
factors.expression_assessment: Given the vol landscape, the fundamental Bullish USD view is best expressed through spot rather than options at the tactical horizon. IV is trading at a premium to RV, meaning options buyers are overpaying for protection. For directional USD longs, spot positions (or short EUR/USD, short GBP/USD) are more capital-efficient than buying USD calls. If the view is that DXY breaks above 102, a spot long DXY with a stop below 99.5 (200d MA) offers a clean risk-reward. The exception is USD/JPY, where the BoJ hike tail risk makes options potentially attractive for bearish JPY expressions (USD/JPY call spreads to capture carry + upside while limiting BoJ hike risk). For the bearish strategic view, the IV premium makes buying EUR/USD or AUD/USD calls relatively expensive — consider risk reversals (sell USD calls/buy USD puts) to take advantage of the skew favoring USD call premium.

tactical.analysis: Analyzing from the supply side (as required for USD dossiers): the Fed's balance sheet at $6.61T with QT ended since December 2025 represents a neutral-to-supportive liquidity backdrop. All Treasury principal is being rolled over and MBS principal reinvested into T-bills, meaning the Fed is no longer actively draining reserves. Reserve balances at $3.0-3.1T are comfortably above the estimated minimum comfortable level (~$2.5T), so no liquidity stress is emerging from the balance sheet. SOFR at 3.63% (-2bp to IORB of 3.65%) is well-behaved — the year-end spike to 3.87% on December 31 was seasonal and normalized immediately. No Fed swap line activations in March suggest foreign central banks are not experiencing acute USD funding stress despite the conflict. The RRP facility usage near zero (from $2.4T at peak) indicates private markets are absorbing available USD liquidity efficiently. However, the Iran conflict introduces a subtle supply-side dynamic: the TGA at $871B is elevated, meaning Treasury has substantial cash reserves and does not need to issue aggressively in the near term — reducing T-bill supply and supporting front-end liquidity. The dollar smile framework places USD firmly on the left side (risk-off) where strong global demand for USD funding reinforces USD strength. Quarter-end (March 31) typically sees elevated USD demand for settlement purposes, which has provided an additional tactical tailwind.

tactical.what_is_priced: Money markets are pricing normal liquidity conditions — SOFR well-behaved, no basis swap stress visible at the aggregate level. The tactical risk would be a sudden basis swap widening if the Iran conflict triggers oil-payment-related USD demand from energy importers (Japan, Europe), but this has not materialized yet. The dollar smile's risk-off USD demand channel is the dominant liquidity dynamic.

strategic.analysis: The strategic liquidity picture is genuinely mixed. On the supportive side, QT has ended, meaning the Fed is no longer actively draining reserves — a structural positive for global USD liquidity that was a headwind for 2.5 years (June 2022-December 2025). The Fed balance sheet at $6.61T is stabilizing, and reserve balances should remain adequate through 2026. The RRP near-zero means the buffer has been fully drained and absorbed by private markets, so there is no additional liquidity injection from this source — a neutral factor. On the less supportive side, several developments could tighten global USD conditions over 3-6 months: Treasury issuance remains heavy ($10T+ rollover in 2026 at elevated yields), which absorbs private liquidity; if the Iran conflict persists, petrodollar recycling could shift (Gulf states pricing oil in alternative currencies, a trend China and BRICS are encouraging); the ECB's EUREP facility enhancement (February 14) signals European concern about USD funding access — not stress yet, but precautionary positioning; and the Warsh succession raises questions about whether the Fed will maintain the swap line infrastructure that Powell built — any perceived retreat would be negative for global USD liquidity confidence. The dollar smile framework creates strategic ambiguity: if the conflict resolves and growth stabilizes (goldilocks middle), USD weakens on improved global liquidity confidence; if the conflict persists or a recession develops, USD stays strong on risk-off demand.

strategic.what_is_priced: Markets are not pricing any systemic USD liquidity risk — SOFR well-behaved, no basis swap stress, no swap line usage. The strategic risk is in the tail: if the Iran conflict triggers a broader payment system fragmentation (alternative oil settlement currencies) or if the Warsh-led Fed signals reduced commitment to global USD liquidity infrastructure, the repricing would be significant. Probability of systemic USD liquidity stress: approximately 10-15%.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical Bullish reflects the supportive liquidity backdrop (QT ended, SOFR stable, dollar smile risk-off demand). Strategic Neutral reflects the genuine uncertainty about whether the liquidity environment remains supportive if the conflict resolves (removing USD demand) or if structural shifts in the global payment system begin to erode USD's funding privilege.

weighted_score.interpretation: A net weighted score of +0.542 is a moderate Bullish signal. Scores above +0.30 typically map to Bullish; above +0.60 to Strong Bullish. At +0.542, the signal is solidly Bullish but not at maximum strength — consistent with Conviction 3/5.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Six of ten factors are Bullish, four are Neutral, and none are Bearish, indicating broad directional agreement with moderate confidence. The concentration of bullish signals in the three highest-weighted factors (Policy 0.24, Inflation 0.18, Liquidity 0.11) produces a strong core signal, tempered by Neutral positioning and external balance factors.

tactical.rationale: The tactical USD bull case rests on three reinforcing pillars. First, the Iran war safe-haven bid (Factor 7, highest confidence at 80%) is the most powerful near-term driver, creating risk-off USD demand that has already produced a +2.8% DXY rally in March. Second, the re-acceleration of core inflation (Factor 2: Core PCE 3.1%, ISM Prices Paid 70.5) has forced hawkish Fed repricing that supports the rate differential channel (Factor 1: +288bp vs JPY, +363bp vs CHF, +163bp vs EUR). Third, the risk-off sentiment (Factor 3: VIX 30+, S&P -8.7% from highs, AAII bearish 50%) and the dollar smile framework reinforce USD as the destination currency in the current environment. These three factors — geopolitics, inflation/rates, and risk sentiment — account for 0.52 of the 0.54 net weighted score, demonstrating the concentration of the bull case. The neutralizing factors (Positioning at speed-of-repositioning caution, External Balance structurally negative but tactically inert, Fiscal risks priced) are not bearish — they are simply not generating a directional signal, which limits conviction to 3/5 rather than 4 or 5. The path of least resistance over 2-4 weeks is DXY grinding toward 102, supported by conflict premium and hawkish data flow (March NFP April 3, March CPI April 10). The risk to the bull case is a ceasefire announcement, which would trigger rapid de-risking of USD longs — but the probability of this within 2 weeks is low (~15%). Actionability is high: spot short EUR/USD and short GBP/USD offer the cleanest tactical expressions given the energy-vulnerability differential.

g10_ranking.commentary: USD ranks behind the two commodity-exporting hiking CBs (AUD, NOK) because their carry advantage is expanding while USD's is static. USD beats all energy importers and low-rate currencies. JPY ranks weakest due to energy import vulnerability and persistent carry outflows despite BoJ normalization.

base_case.path: Step-wise grind higher supported by 200d MA floor at 99.0, attracted by 102 resistance. War-headline-driven volatility punctuates the trend.

conditions: Oil surges to $130+; VIX spikes above 35
Oil drops to $80-85; VIX collapses to 18-22

tactical.path_expectation: DXY is expected to grind higher in a step-wise fashion over the next 2-4 weeks, supported by the base of the 200-day MA at 99.0 and attracted by the 102 resistance level. The path will be punctuated by event-driven spikes (Iran headlines, NFP, CPI releases) rather than a smooth trend, given VIX at 30+. Pullbacks to 99.5-100.0 are buying opportunities in the base case. A break above 102 would be a momentum-acceleration event that shifts the path from grind to impulse. The shape is a staircase higher with war-headline-driven volatility.

increase_to_5_if: DXY breaks above 102 AND Core PCE remains at or above 3.0% AND March NFP negative again
VIX above 35 sustained AND Fed minutes reveal hike discussion

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly positive — the payoff distribution is skewed in USD's favor because upside scenarios (escalation) have a lower probability but higher payoff than the downside (ceasefire). The expected value of +75 pips represents a positive-EV tactical trade.

weighted_score.interpretation: A net weighted score of -0.300 is a moderate Bearish signal. Scores between -0.20 and -0.40 map to Bearish (not Strong Bearish). Four factors are Bearish, six are Neutral, and none are Bullish.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: The strategic view is one of accumulated headwinds rather than a single dominant bearish driver, which limits conviction to 3/5. The bearish signal is distributed across Policy (-0.132), External Balance (-0.096), Fiscal (-0.039), and Positioning (-0.033) — no single factor dominates.

strategic.rationale: The strategic USD bear case is driven by the convergence of four structural headwinds that gain weight at the 3-6 month horizon. First, the rate differential compression (Factor 1, weight 0.24) is the most impactful: BoJ normalizing toward 1.00-1.25%, RBA at 4.10%+, Norges Bank about to hike, ECB and RBNZ repricing toward hikes — the global rate trajectory is converging upward toward the US, eroding carry that has supported USD since 2022. Second, the external balance (Factor 5, weight 0.16) becomes the second-most influential factor at the strategic horizon: twin deficits of -2.4% GDP (current account) and -5.8% GDP (fiscal) require continuous foreign financing that is increasingly competed for by higher-yielding G10 alternatives. Third, positioning (Factor 4) is bearish because the war-driven USD long build is temporary and will reverse as conflict premium fades, with the EUR's record positioning underweight creating snap-back potential. Fourth, fiscal risk (Factor 8) exerts gradual downward pressure through elevated term premium and growing investor discomfort with the $39T debt trajectory. The six Neutral factors — Inflation/Growth, Risk, Guidance, Event Risk, Market Structure, Liquidity — reflect genuine uncertainty that dampens conviction. The most important of these is Guidance (weight 0.12), where the Warsh Chair transition creates a 2-4 month calibration period that could either reinforce or undermine USD depending on how credibly Warsh communicates. The path of least resistance over 3-6 months is a gradual DXY decline toward 96-98 as the conflict premium fades and differential compression plays out. The key risk to the bear case is a prolonged Iran conflict (oil above $100 through Q3) combined with persistent US inflation outperformance that prevents the Fed from cutting.

g10_ranking.commentary: USD drops from 3rd tactically to 8th strategically, reflecting the shift from safe-haven premium to structural headwinds. AUD and NOK remain top-ranked due to hiking cycles and commodity exports. EUR benefits from de-escalation recovery + ECB tightening. JPY benefits from BoJ normalization narrowing the wide rate gap.

conditions: Fed delivers one cut in Q3
base_case.path: Two-phase decline: Phase 1 (April-June) DXY holds 99-102 on residual conflict premium; Phase 2 (July-September) 3-5% decline as oil normalizes and positioning reverses.

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is expected to unfold in two phases. Phase 1 (April-June): DXY remains elevated at 99-102, supported by conflict premium and hawkish data flow, but with diminishing upward momentum as the conflict ages and markets adjust. Phase 2 (July-September): Conflict de-escalation triggers a 3-5% DXY decline over 4-8 weeks as oil normalizes, risk appetite recovers, positioning reverses, and differential compression accelerates. The shape is a spike-and-fade — a conflict-driven overshoot followed by a gravitational pull back to fundamental value. The transition from Phase 1 to Phase 2 is the key inflection point, likely triggered by a ceasefire or credible negotiation.

increase_to_5_if: Iran ceasefire confirmed AND BoJ hikes to 1.00% AND US unemployment above 4.7%
Fed signals multiple cuts AND foreign official UST purchases decline
decrease_to_1_if: Iran escalation plus US stagflation avoidance
All peer CBs pivot dovish simultaneously

risk_reward.downside_case_loss: DXY 100.5 to 100.5 = 0 pips (flat; opportunity cost only in upside scenario)
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: Excellent — base case offers 3.5% with limited upside risk (USD upside capped at current levels in 25% scenario). Probability-weighted EV: -350 pips expected USD depreciation.
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Strongly positive for the bearish view — the risk is flat-to-mild positive USD, the reward is 3.5-7.0% USD decline. The distribution is skewed in favor of the bearish thesis because the conflict premium is inherently temporary while the structural headwinds are persistent.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Most major banks (Goldman, MUFG, RBC, ING, Deutsche Bank) project DXY at 93-98 by year-end 2026, driven by Fed easing, differential compression, and structural overvaluation unwinding. EUR/USD consensus is 1.20-1.25 by Q4 2026. USD/JPY consensus is 138-150 by Q4 2026.
consensus_divergence.our_view: We agree directionally with the consensus strategic bear case but differ on timing and magnitude. Our tactical bullish view (DXY grinding to 101-102 through May) is approximately 3-5% above where consensus models would place the USD in Q2. Our strategic base case of DXY 96-98 by September is less bearish than the most aggressive forecasters (MUFG's 93-95, Goldman's mid-90s).
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Tactical: +3-5% above consensus for Q2 2026. Strategic: 0-3% above the most bearish forecasters; broadly aligned with median consensus for year-end.
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Consensus underweights two factors: (a) the duration of the conflict premium — most bank forecasts were issued before the Iran war and have not been updated to reflect the conflict premium, and (b) the possibility that global tightening creates a more symmetric rate environment rather than a purely USD-negative one. Conversely, consensus correctly identifies structural overvaluation and twin deficits as dominant long-run headwinds — we agree on the direction, just not the speed. The Warsh Chair transition also introduces a wildcard that consensus has not fully incorporated.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: The payoff is modest — we capture the tactical USD upside (1-2%) that consensus misses, then ride the strategic decline with better timing. The total return from timing the top rather than selling now could be 2-4% additional.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If consensus is right and DXY is already declining, our tactical bullish view loses 1-2% before we join the strategic bearish move. The opportunity cost is modest — missing the first 1-2% of a structural move while collecting carry.

cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: EUR is the most vulnerable G10 currency to the current regime: energy importer (TTF gas vulnerability), ECB constrained by inflation/growth tradeoff, record positioning underweight (10th percentile), and the largest DXY weight (57.6%). Target: 1.12-1.13. The carry is modest but positive for USD (+163bp), and the positioning momentum is strongly in USD's favor on this pair.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: AUD combines RBA hiking (+48bp carry advantage over USD), commodity export benefit from elevated prices, 9-year positioning highs reflecting fundamental strength, and 7% PPP undervaluation. Target: 0.72-0.74 by September. AUD is uniquely positioned to benefit from both conflict resolution (risk-on recovery) and conflict persistence (commodity export revenue).

relative_value_pairs.thesis: BoJ normalization + shrinking UST-JGB spread + JPY positioning at 85th percentile bearishness = potential for a violent JPY rally on any BoJ hike catalyst. Risk: carry is expensive (-288bp). Best expressed with a stop above 162 and patience for the 148-153 target.
Strategic entry for the positioning snap-back trade. EUR's crash from 90th to 10th percentile creates spring-loaded upside potential once conflict premium fades. Target 1.20+.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical Bullish and Strategic Bearish — a classic cross-horizon divergence driven by the distinction between transient (conflict premium) and persistent (structural headwinds) forces.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The tactical horizon is dominated by the Iran conflict premium (safe-haven flows, oil-driven inflation, risk-off sentiment) which is a finite, exogenous shock, while the strategic horizon is dominated by structural headwinds (rate differential compression, twin deficits, REER overvaluation, reserve diversification) that reassert once the conflict premium fades. The two views are not contradictory; they are sequential phases of a larger narrative.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: May-July 2026, triggered by either Iran ceasefire/de-escalation, DXY failure at 102 resistance, or cumulative data evidence of US growth slowdown forcing dovish Fed recalibration.
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: The most likely catalyst is a ceasefire or credible negotiation in the Iran conflict, which would simultaneously remove the safe-haven bid, collapse oil prices, normalize risk appetite, and trigger positioning reversal — a multi-channel USD-negative shock.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Position tactically long USD (2-4 week horizon) with tight stops and explicit targets (DXY 101-102). Prepare strategic short USD positions for initiation at the inflection point. Do not hold tactical longs into the strategic horizon — the transition will be rapid once it begins.

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: Forward points imply gradual EUR appreciation driven by the positive EUR/USD carry from the rate differential. Our tactical view diverges from the forward-implied path by 145-267 pips over 1-3 months — this is the tactical USD-bullish edge. Our strategic view exceeds the forward-implied 1.1551 by 349 pips — this is the strategic USD-bearish edge. Both edges are tradeable and exceed typical transaction costs.

Section 122 tariff 150-day expiry clock (SCOTUS ruling was Feb 20; 150-day limit creates July expiry)

seasonality.pattern: April has historically been a modestly negative month for DXY (average -0.3% over the past 20 years), driven by tax-season repatriation flows and Q2 portfolio rebalancing.
seasonality.historical_magnitude: -0.3% average DXY decline in April over 20 years; mild and unreliable
seasonality.current_alignment: The seasonal pattern may provide a minor headwind at month-end but is unlikely to dominate in the current war-driven regime. The early-April tax-season USD bid could provide a small tailwind that aligns with the tactical bullish view. By May, the seasonal pattern turns neutral-to-negative for USD.

assumptions: Core PCE (3.1%) is used as the primary inflation reference for Fed policy assessment, per the Fed's stated preference, despite the more benign Core CPI reading (2.5%). The CPI-PCE divergence is noted but PCE is given analytical priority.

Cross-currency basis swap levels (3M EUR/USD, JPY/USD, GBP/USD) were not available from public sources — these require Bloomberg terminal access. Factor 10 analysis relies on proxies (SOFR-IORB spread, swap line usage, RRP levels) to assess USD funding conditions.

25-delta risk reversal levels and OTC IV term structures were not available from public sources. Estimates are based on exchange-traded futures IV and directional inference from positioning and spot dynamics.
PPP valuations use Big Mac Index (February 2026 vintage) as the primary reference. OECD PPP is cited where available. PPP is treated as a long-term gravitational pull, not a tactical signal.

The base case assumes Iran conflict de-escalation by June-July 2026 based on historical analogue duration, Trump's stated desire to negotiate, and domestic political pressure. This is a probabilistic assumption (55% base case), not a prediction.
EUR/USD forward points are estimated from policy rate differentials rather than actual OTC market quotes. These are indicative and may differ from tradeable forward levels by 5-10 pips.

Previous dossier comparison uses the March 11, 2026 USD dossier retrieved via conversation history search. Comparison is on final outputs only (factor stances, conviction, weighted scores), per the framework requirement of deferred retrieval.
